query: best management for pediatric croup including role of racemic epinephrine and corticosteroids

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Croup is a common respiratory illness that affects the trachea, larynx, and bronchi, leading to inspiratory stridor and barking cough. This infection is a self-limited disease typically observed in children younger than 5. The clinical spectrum includes laryngotracheitis, laryngotracheobronchitis, and laryngotracheobronchopneumonitis. Most children with croup improve with a single dose of dexamethasone and supportive management. However, a few with severe symptoms require racemic epinephrine in addition to dexamethasone. Uncommon complications include bacterial tracheitis, pneumonia, pulmonary edema, and, rarely, death. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of croup, emphasizing the importance of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition. As an upper respiratory condition, stridor is the presenting symptom, which must be differentiated from wheezing caused by lower respiratory conditions. This activity emphasizes the importance of distinguishing between upper and lower respiratory signs and symptoms, as this differentiation guides appropriate management. The activity also discusses the differential diagnosis of croup and its management, highlighting the need for prompt differentiation of conditions, especially severe and rapidly deteriorating conditions such as epiglottitis, which require intensive care admission and actions to secure the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: Stridor in Children -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Foreign body aspirations should be considered in cases where symptoms develop acutely, such as sudden coughing and choking in a previously healthy child. In the case of croup, beta-agonists should be avoided as they may potentially exacerbate upper airway obstruction. Antibiotics are necessary for bacterial tracheitis and epiglottitis. Both steroids and racemic epinephrine have proven to be effective for managing croup. Surgical drainage is necessary for retropharyngeal and peritonsillar abscesses. Severe laryngomalacia, laryngeal stenosis, critical tracheal stenosis, laryngeal and tracheal tumors, and foreign body aspiration require surgical correction.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheobronchitis -- Treatment / Management -- Nebulized Epinephrine
retrieved_docs.content: Racemic epinephrine 2.25% 0.05 mL/kg nebulized with a maximum of 5 mL or L-epinephrine 0.5 mL/kg nebulized with a maximum of 5 mL is reserved for moderate and severe croup. Admit patients requiring more than 1 to 2 doses of nebulized epinephrine in the emergency department.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with moderate croup should be evaluated and monitored. These patients often require the administration of racemic epinephrine in addition to dexamethasone and supportive care. Patients can be discharged if  the following criteria are met: They are improved 3 hours after the last nebulized racemic epinephrine treatment Pulse oximetry reading is normal They tolerate oral fluids They have a non-toxic appearance, normal color, and no stridor at rest They have reliable caretakers who understand symptoms requiring a return visit They can return for close follow-up if needed

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Westley score, patients with severe croup or those showing persistent symptoms after 2 or more doses of racemic epinephrine should be considered for extended observation or hospital admission. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Supraglottic Airway Obstruction -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids have proven to be of benefit for causes related to edema and inflammation of the upper airway in conditions such as croup. The mechanism of action includes anti-inflammatory and reduction of edema. Racemic epinephrine via nebulized route has both alpha and beta action and causes upper and lower airway lumen dilation, which improves air entry and results in a more laminar airflow.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the Westley croup score, treatment depends on the severity of signs and symptoms. Children with mild croup receive a single dose of dexamethasone, symptomatic treatment, and supportive care. Children with moderate-to-severe croup are given nebulized epinephrine and dexamethasone. [14] All patients with diminished oxygen saturation should receive supplemental oxygen. Moderate-to-severe cases require observation and admission if the symptoms do not improve. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Epinephrine
retrieved_docs.content: Nebulized racemic epinephrine has been shown to improve symptom scores within 30 minutes, but its effects may diminish after 2 hours in patients with moderate-to-severe croup, who, therefore, require a prolonged period of observation after receiving racemic epinephrine. If symptoms improve, they can be discharged home after 4 hours of observation, with instructions for close follow-up. Administering approximately 0.5 mL/kg of L-epinephrine 1:1000 using a nebulizer appears more effective than racemic epinephrine at 2 hours due to its longer duration of action. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with moderate-to-severe symptoms should receive prompt care, including racemic epinephrine, steroids, oxygen as needed, and supportive management. Recurrent and atypical croup merits special attention to determine the underlying cause and appropriate treatment. Emergency nurses are often the first to evaluate patients and must recognize those who require urgent interventions. A collaborative team of healthcare providers, nurses, and pharmacists must be engaged in ordering, verifying the dosage of medications, checking for drug-drug interactions, administering medications, educating families, and reporting changes for effective closed-loop communication. In severe cases of croup, involvement of other skilled professionals such as ENT specialists, anesthesiologists, intensivists, and operating room staff is critical for securing the patient's airway and providing ongoing care. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Oral dexamethasone is the preferred treatment for children with croup because it is effective, widely available, safe, and easily administered. A single dose is sufficient for most children. If symptoms persist for 3 to 4 days after the first dose of dexamethasone, a second dose can be administered.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone, a glucocorticosteroid, helps accelerate recovery, lowers the need for additional medical care, and shortens the length of hospital stay.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of mild croup subside in the majority of children within 2 days. However, a few patients have symptoms that persist for up to 1 week. Moderate-to-severe croup symptoms need treatment with epinephrine in addition to glucocorticoids. Complications such as pneumonia, pulmonary edema, and secondary bacterial infection may occur, leading to tracheitis, requiring antibiotics. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: The oral route is less invasive than an injection, making it more comfortable for children already experiencing respiratory distress. However, in cases where the child is unable to swallow, is vomiting, or has severe respiratory distress, intramuscular or intravenous dexamethasone may be necessary. Oral medication is also less costly and may be prescribed for home use in cases of mild croup.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with croup are often assessed by primary care clinicians or in emergency departments. Educating healthcare providers is essential to ensure they understand that croup is generally a self-limiting condition, with most patients achieving full recovery through supportive care alone and having an excellent prognosis. Many may benefit from pharmacological therapy in the form of a single dose of dexamethasone. A community-based study of children with croup found no difference in symptom scores between 3 daily doses of prednisolone (2 mg/kg) and 1 dose of dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg). [29] In another study on pediatric patients with croup, steroids effectively reduced croup symptoms, shortened hospital admission stays, and reduced the need for follow-up visits. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheobronchitis -- Treatment / Management -- Observation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with croup should be observed for a minimum of 3 hours after the completion of each dose of nebulized racemic epinephrine. Watch for worsening or recurrence of symptoms, including persistent stridor at rest, increased work of breathing, and hypoxia, during the observation period. Admit patients requiring more than one or two doses of nebulized epinephrine in the emergency department.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheobronchitis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Croup is managed by an interprofessional team that includes a pediatrician, emergency department physician, nurse practitioner, and radiologist. The diagnosis can be difficult because there is no specific test for the disorder. The goal of treatment is to decrease airway obstruction. Treatment includes nebulized epinephrine, corticosteroids, and at least 3 hours of observation after the last dose of epinephrine. Supplemental oxygen via blow-by or nasal cannula can also be used for hypoxic patients with croup. The majority of patients can be safely discharged home from the emergency department but the caretaker must be informed about the importance of avoiding smoking in the home. Other factors to consider before discharge are that the caretaker recognizes an improvement in the child’s symptoms, the caretaker has adequate transportation to return if necessary, and there are no significant social issue concerns. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 36
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network of inhaled, intranasal, and systemic glucocorticoid therapies used for obstructive airway diseases and related conditions. Adrenergic inhalants combined with corticosteroids (excluding anticholinergics) form a therapeutic class that includes fixed‑dose products such as albuterol + beclomethasone, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone (Momesalic), and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate; budesonide, Momesalic, and fluticasone furoate are the corticosteroid components of these combinations. The same corticosteroids—budesonide, Momesalic, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate—appear in topical nasal preparations and in the class of glucocorticoid inhalants for obstructive airway disease, which also encompasses systemic glucocorticoid derivatives such as hydrocortisone sodium succinate, prednisolone acetate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and the child agent triamcinolone diacetate. These agents are employed with long‑acting β‑agonists (formoterol, vilanterol) or short‑acting β‑agonists (albuterol) to treat asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and allergic rhinitis. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, administered intravenously, is additionally indicated for acute bronchial or asthmatic exacerbations and for circulatory collapse, illustrating the therapeutic continuum from topical anti‑inflammatory inhalation to systemic emergency management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 119
retrieved_docs.content: Cromolyn is classified within the Miscellaneous Drugs category and appears as an active ingredient in several combination products, including those that also contain albuterol or isoproterenol. The ophthalmic formulation of cromolyn sodium (40 mg/mL) represents its active moiety, and some products list a combined active ingredient of isoproterenol + cromolyn. Pharmacologically, cromolyn exerts physiologic effects that decrease both histamine and leukotriene activity, mechanisms that underlie its utility in managing mast cell–related disorders such as mast cell hyperplasia. Although cromolyn is commonly employed to prevent exercise‑induced asthma, it may be contraindicated in certain asthmatic conditions, highlighting the need for careful patient selection. Within the same Miscellaneous Drugs group, chlorbutanol is listed as a child drug and is linked to the broader class “alkoholy” through an inverse is‑a relationship, suggesting a reciprocal classification. Alkoholy also shows inverse is‑a connections with heptaminol and nelfinavir, indicating overlapping or related chemical families. Additionally, metyrosine is identified as another child of the Miscellaneous Drugs category. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is noted for its therapeutic role in acute medical emergencies; it may be administered to treat both asthma‑related crises and circulatory collapse, reflecting its potent glucocorticoid and hemodynamic support properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1585
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolon and methoxsalen are pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat الفطريات الفطرية (fungal infections). However, both methylprednisolon and methoxsalen are contraindicated in رضيع (infants), indicating that these drugs should not be administered to this vulnerable age group. This underscores the importance of selecting alternative, age‑appropriate therapies when managing fungal infections in infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 24
retrieved_docs.content: Cromolyn is an active ingredient present in several combination products, including those that pair it with albuterol or isoproterenol, and these formulations are classified under the Miscellaneous Drugs category, indicating that cromolyn is a child of this broader drug class. The specific formulation cromolyn sodium 40 mg/mL ophthalmic solution represents the active moiety of cromolyn. Pharmacologically, cromolyn produces physiologic effects by decreasing histamine activity and decreasing leukotriene activity, mechanisms that are relevant to conditions such as mast‑cell hyperplasia. Clinically, cromolyn is used to prevent exercise‑induced asthma and to treat food‑related allergic reactions, while it is contraindicated in patients with an underlying asthmatic (bronchial) condition. Combination products that contain both isoproterenol and cromolyn list these two agents as the active ingredients. Within the same Miscellaneous Drugs group, chlorbutanol is also a child drug, and metyrosine is listed as another child of this category. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, a corticosteroid preparation, is employed in the management of severe asthma (bronchial) episodes, circulatory collapse, and other acute inflammatory states. Quercetin, a flavonoid, may be used to treat food allergy, aligning with cromolyn’s role in managing similar allergic conditions. Together, cromolyn, hydrocortisone sodium succinate, chlorbutanol, metyrosine, and quercetin illustrate a network of agents within the miscellaneous drug class, each linked by therapeutic indications related to allergic, inflammatory, and emergency cardiovascular or respiratory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 65
retrieved_docs.content: Its pharmacologic actions are characterized by inhibition of histamine and leukotriene release, providing antiallergic and anti‑inflammatory effects; clinically it is used to prevent exercise‑induced asthma. Cromolyn is contraindicated in the Arabic‑described condition حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة and is associated with mast‑cell hyperplasia (كثرة الخلايا البدينة), warranting caution in patients with excessive mast‑cell proliferation. Within the same nasal‑preparation hierarchy, antiallergic nasal products are also classified as a type of decongestant, grouping them with other topical nasal decongestants and systemic nasal decongestants under the broader “NASAL PREPARATIONS” category. Levocabastine exemplifies a topical antiallergic nasal preparation that lacks corticosteroids. The “Other antiallergics” ATC subgroup additionally includes the second‑generation antihistamine cetirizin, whose specific dosage form cetirizine hydrochloride 1 mg/mL oral solution is catalogued as a child entity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 182
retrieved_docs.content: a β‑adrenergic agonist, providing both bronchodilatory (via isoproterenol) and anti‑inflammatory (via cromolyn) actions. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, a systemic corticosteroid, is indicated for the treatment of both حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (asthmatic exacerbations) and الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse or shock). Its anti‑inflammatory and hemodynamic support properties complement the mast‑cell‑stabilizing effects of cromolyn in acute severe asthma and in states of hemodynamic instability. Together, these relationships highlight a therapeutic network in which cromolyn, alone or in combination with albuterol, isoproterenol, or other agents, modulates mast‑cell‑mediated inflammation, prevents exercise‑induced bronchospasm, and must be used judiciously in severe asthma, while hydrocortisone sodium succinate provides broader anti‑inflammatory and circulatory support for severe asthma attacks and shock.

This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 445
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergics inhalants in combination with corticosteroids or other drugs, excluding anticholinergics, constitute a therapeutic class that includes several fixed‑dose combinations such as albuterol + beclomethasone, budesonide / formoterol, formoterol / mometasone, and vilanterol + fluticasone furoate. Within the formoterol / mometasone combination, the corticosteroid Momesalic is the component that provides the anti‑inflammatory effect. Fluticasone furoate, which is a component of the vilanterol + fluticasone furoate combination, is also classified as a corticosteroid nasal preparation for topical use and as a glucocorticoid inhalant for obstructive airway disease. Accordingly, the nasal preparation class and the inhalant glucocorticoid class share several members, including Momesalic, ciclesonide, flunisolide, and fluticasone furoate. The glucocorticoid inhalant category further encompasses related steroid agents that are listed as “children” of this class: dexamethasone sodium phosphate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific corticosteroid and adrenergic agents are organized into combination products and therapeutic groups used for airway inflammation, allergic rhinitis, and obstructive lung diseases, supporting integrated prescribing and patient‑care strategies.

Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, although a corticosteroid, is employed for severe inflammatory and hemodynamic emergencies, including growth‑related disorders and circulatory collapse. Overall, the community’s knowledge maps a complex ATC‑based classification linking nasal, inhaled and combination antiallergic therapies (azelastine, antazolin, levocabastine, olopatadine, nedokromil, cromolyn, cetirizine, honokiol) with related decongestants, inhaled corticosteroids, and safety considerations such as mast‑cell activity and contraindications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2367
retrieved_docs.content: The term “nos” is classified as a child of “other upper respiratory disease,” and “other upper respiratory disease” in turn is a child of the broader category “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر” (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere). The clinical procedure “control of epistaxis” is performed directly at the site identified as “nos” and its primary focus is “الرُعاف” (epistaxis). Additionally, “الرُعاف” is recognized as a child condition within the overarching group “اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر.” This hierarchy links the nonspecific upper respiratory condition (“nos”) to a specific nasal disorder category and highlights that the management of epistaxis targets the “nos” site while addressing the condition “الرُعاف,” which itself belongs to the same broader nasal disorder classification.
